title: practical english usage - Part 354
content:
Hope and be afraid are always used in the first structure.

‘ Will it rain?' ‘ I don't expect so?

‘ You won't be here tomorrow? ‘ I don't suppose so?

‘ We won't be in time for the train.' ‘ No, I suppose not?

‘ Did you win? ‘ I'm afraid not.' affirmative verb + not We   can make these expressions negative in two ways.

2  negative       structures or                                 not ‘ You're late.' ‘ I know.'  I know that, (     I-know so.) So is not used after know (►  504).

Empty your pockets. Ah,  I thought so! You've been stealing chocolate again.

Note the special use of I thought so to mean 'my suspicions were correct'.

vocabulary' •  588  so that and in order that She's staying here for six months so that she can perfect her English.

verbs such as can or will; may is more formal.

in order that, especially in an informal style. They  are often followed by auxiliary These structures are used to talk about purpose. So that is more common than 1 purpose 588  so   that     and   in   order      that If he so much  as looks at another woman, I'll kill him.

He didn't so much  as say thank you, after all we'd done for him.

In negative and /-clauses, so much  as can be used to mean ‘ even’ .

It's not so much  that I don't want to go, but I just haven't got time.

It wasn't so much  his appearance I liked as his personality.

clarifications.

We   can use not so much  ... as or not so much  . .. but to make corrections and 4 special     structures        with    so  much I wish you didn 't smoke so much.

So much  can be used as an adverb.

3   so   much      as  an   adverb I was expecting a few phone calls, but not so many.

/ can't eat all that meat - there's so much!

We   can drop a noun after so much/many, if the meaning is clear.

2  so   much/many            without      a  noun She's so much  more beautiful now.

But so much  is used before comparatives (►  207).

You're so beautiful, (     You're so much  beautiful.) We   use so, not so much,  to modify adjectives and adverbs (►  584.5).

(     ... so-much children-.. .) She had so many children that she didn't know what to do.

I had never seen so much  food in my life.

used with plurals.

many (►   1 65). So much  is used with singular (uncountable) nouns; so many is The  difference between so much  and so many is the same as between much  and 1  the   difference 587  so   much      and   so   many ►    597 and so      such She promised mew.

So cannot be used after all verbs of saying. We   cannot say, for example, 3 other     verbs Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary’  •  589  so-and-so; so-so When will I get married - this year, next year, sometime, never?

Let's have dinner together sometime next week.

It can also be written as two words: some time.

Sometime (/'sAmtaim/) refers to an indefinite time, usually in the future.

She's lived in Italy for some time, so she speaks Italian quite well.

I'm afraid it'll take some time to repair your car.

Some time (with two stresses: /,sʌm  'taɪm/) means ‘ quite a long time’ .

590 some      time,     sometime           and   sometimes 'Was the concert any good? * So-so.* not 'How are you feeling?’ 'So-so. ’ (     .. .So-and-so.) This informal expression means ‘ neither good nor bad.’ 2 so-so She's an old so-and-so.

It can also replace a swear word or an insult.

What's happened to old so-and-so? (= ... what’ s his name?) This informal expression is used when one cannot remember a name.

589  so-a   nd-so;     so-  so For lest meaning 'so that. .. not’ , ►Sil.

I or the infinitive structures in order to and so as to, ►     1 12.2.   For so... that expressing result, ►    58-1.-1.

He built a chain of castles so that he might control the whole country.

They met on a Saturday in order that everybody should be free to attend.

I took my golf clubs so that I could play on Sunday.

Emily talked to the shy girl so that she wouldn't feel left out.

verbs after so that / in order that. Might is possible in a very formal style.

In sentences about the past, would, could or should are generally used with 3  past    structures hiding things.
